mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz SLC 220d or 250d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM651 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,000 supply only; a reconditioned OM654 costs £3,500–£7,500. Used units are available from £2,000–£3,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your SLC.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz SLC 220d / 250d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 is a proven workhorse but suffers from timing chain stretch and injector failure. The OM654 is more modern and efficient but has reports of rocker arm bearing wear and EGR issues. Both demand diligent maintenance with correct MB-spec oil.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz SLC 220d and 250d?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM651 (220d): timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start), Delphi piezo injector failure (rough idle, limp mode), DPF pressure sensor failure. OM654 (250d): rocker arm bearing wear (tapping from top of engine), EGR clogging, turbocharger wear.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz SLC 220d / 250d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: SLC values remain strong — a well-kept R172 is often worth £12,000–£25,000+. A £2,500–£7,500 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz SLC 220d or 250d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. R172 SLC 220d (2016–2019) uses OM651; R172 SLC 250d (2019–2020) uses OM654. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £1,200–£2,500. The OM651's timing chain is a known issue due to a faulty chain tensioner. If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. At the first sign of rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch and Tensioner Wear (OM651 — 220d)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or metallic noise on cold start lasting 1–3 seconds, engine warning light, poor timing performance, eventual chain slip
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£5,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: this is one of the most common OM651 issues

problems.recommendation: The OM651's timing chain can stretch due to a faulty chain tensioner, causing premature elongation. Early production engines (up to 2012) are particularly susceptible. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Delphi Piezo Injector Failure (OM651 — 220d, Pre-2012)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, limp mode, hesitation on acceleration, high-pressure rail faults, poor fuel economy
problems.recommendation: Early OM651 engines use Delphi piezo injectors which are the most common failure point. If you experience rough idle or limp mode, have the fuel system inspected. Replacing injectors is significantly cheaper than dealing with the consequences of a seized engine.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector failure guide

problems.title: Rocker Arm Bearing and Valvetrain Wear (OM654 — 250d)
problems.symptoms: Tapping or ticking noise from the top of the engine, reduced power, odd noises, excess exhaust smoke

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: worn rocker arm bearings can lead to catastrophic camshaft damage

problems.recommendation: The OM654 has known issues with failing rocker arm bearings. If left unchecked, this can lead to camshaft damage, requiring extensive and expensive repairs. If you hear tapping from the top of the engine, have the valvetrain inspected immediately. Regular oil changes with the correct MB-spec oil are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full valvetrain failure guide

problems.title: EGR System Clogging (OM654 — 250d)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light
problems.recommendation: The OM654's EGR system can suffer from clogging. Short journeys exacerbate the issue. Regular motorway driving and occasional EGR cleaning can extend the life of the system.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SLC 220d / 250d? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2019 OM654 with comprehensive service history — the newer engine is lighter and more efficient
* OM651 with documented timing chain and injector work — the common weaknesses addressed
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 10,000 miles) — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Later OM651 models (post-2012) with revised injectors
avoidIf: * OM651 with no proof of timing chain work — pre-2012 cars are especially at risk
* OM654 with tapping from the top of the engine — rocker arm bearing wear is likely
* Any rattling on cold start — timing chain or valvetrain issues are imminent
* Poor service history — both engines are sensitive to oil quality

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The SLC 220d and 250d are powered by two of Mercedes' finest four-cylinder diesels. The OM651 (2016–2019) is a proven workhorse — torquey and capable of high mileage — but timing chain stretch and injector failure are well-documented. The OM654 (2019–2020) is a more modern all-aluminium engine, but rocker arm bearing wear and EGR issues have been reported. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and both engines will reward you with effortless torque and impressive economy.

cta.label: → Compare SLC 220d / 250d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz SLC 220d or 250d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattling from the front of the engine (timing chain warning on OM651 — the most critical check)
2. Service History — including which generation (OM651 or OM654) and evidence of major maintenance
3. Injector Health (OM651) — Any rough idle or limp mode? (pre-2012 Delphi piezo injectors are the most common failure point)
4. Top-End Ticking (OM654) — Any tapping from the top of the engine? (rocker arm bearing wear)
5. EGR System (OM654) — Any loss of power or rough idle? (EGR clogging)
6. Oil Level — Any excessive oil consumption? (valvetrain or turbo issues)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, NOx sensors)
